attributes.stem:
A 62-year-old retired teacher presents with abnormal gait. During the past two years he noticed gradually progressive slowness and clumsiness of walking. He also suffers frequent falls and sometimes finds difficulty initiate walking. His wife reports that he frequently forgets important events, misplaces his personal belongings and once had to call her as he could not find his way home. His medical history includes hypertension, diabetes mellitus and two lacunar strokes that resolved without residual deficits. Two months ago he had a urological opinion for urgent and frequent urination, but no genitourinary abnormalities were found.

The neurologic examination finds a generalised bradykinesia without rigidity in all extremities. Gait initiation is hesitant and his gate is wide-based, slow, with decreased step length and height and less arm swing. His MMSE score is 18. A CT brain is performed (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with magnetic gait (wide base, slow and shuffling) frequent falls, cognitive impairment (memory problems, visuospatial impairment and low MMSE score), and urinary urgency. CT brain shows enlarged lateral ventricle and widened sylvian fissures out of proportion to the cortical sulci. These features are suggestive of normal-pressure hydrocephalus (NPH).

NPH is a potentially reversible syndrome characterised by enlarged cerebral ventricles, with classic triad of cognitive impairment, gait apraxia and urinary incontinence.

When ventriculomegaly is excluded, the NPH symptomatology becomes non-specific as dementia, urinary incontinence, and gait impairment are observed frequently in older patients. In addition, virtually all neurodegenerative diseases lead to enlarged ventricles due to loss of brain tissue. Thus, it is important to include in the differential diagnosis conditions such as Alzheimer’s disease (AD), Parkinson disease (PD), dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB) and vascular dementia (VaD).

According to International Guidelines the following key imaging features should be employed for the diagnosis of NPH:

  • Ventricular enlargement with Evans’s index >0.3 (the ratio of maximum width of the frontal horns of the lateral ventricles and maximal internal diameter of skull at the same level on axial CT or MRI images)

  • Absence of macroscopic obstruction to CSF flow.

  • At least one of these supporting features:

    • Enlarged temporal horns of the lateral ventricles not entirely due to hippocampus atrophy;

    • Callosal angle of 40° or greater

    • Periventricular signal changes on CT and MRI due to altered brain water content and not entirely attributable to microvascular ischemic changes or demyelination;

    • Flow void in the Sylvian aqueduct or fourth ventricle on MRI.

Alzheimer’s disease is estimated to account for 50% of cases of dementia. However, AD dementia often precedes and overshadows motor and urinary symptoms. Also, the dilatation of the ventricles is often accompanied by hippocampal atrophy.

Dementia with Lewy bodies is characterised by fluctuation of awareness from day-to-day and signs of parkinsonism such as tremor, rigidity and slowness of movement or poverty of expression. Visual hallucinations or delusions occur frequently. Falls are also common.

In multi-infarct dementia the onset may be abrupt or there may be periods of sudden decline followed by relative stability. Patients may present with signs of a prior stroke or other vascular problems. Decreased mobility and balance problems are common. Memory may initially be preserved with deficits in other cognitive domains e.g. visuo-spatial.

In Parkinson’s disease 40% of people develop Parkinson’s disease dementia (PDD), especially if older at onset. However, dementia symptoms develop after a person has had Parkinson’s disease for many years.
learningPoints: Normal pressure hydrocephalus is a potentially reversible syndrome characterized by the triad of gait disturbance, cognitive impairment, and urinary incontinence, associated with ventriculomegaly and normal cerebrospinal fluid pressure.

CSF tap-test (lumbar puncture with the removal of 30 to 70 mL with assessment of the manifestation before and after the tap) help in diagnosis and determine if the patient has the potential to respond to shunt surgery.
references: Williams MA, Malm J. Diagnosis and Treatment of Idiopathic Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus. Continuum (Minneap Minn). 2016 Apr;22(2 Dementia):579-99. doi: 10.1212/CON.0000000000000305. PMID: 27042909; PMCID: PMC5390935. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27042909/
Image: Nevit Dilmen, CC BY-SA 3.0 https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Patel S, Ditamo M, Mangal R, Gould M, Ganti L. Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus. Cureus. 2023 Feb 18;15(2):e35131. doi: 10.7759/cureus.35131. PMID: 36949988; PMCID: PMC10026533. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36949988/

Das JM, Biagioni MC. Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus. [Updated 2023 Jun 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK542247/
